past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 5 years ago, well controlled
Dental abscess of right lower second molar treated with incision and drainage plus oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 3 weeks ago
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diet-controlled, diagnosed 2 years ago
Remote history of mild traumatic brain injury (concussion) from a cycling accident 8 years ago, fully recovered
allergies: Penicillin (childhood rash, tolerates amoxicillin without issue per allergist evaluation)
family_history: Father: died of lung cancer at age 71
Mother: alive, age 76, history of multiple sclerosis diagnosed at age 40, currently wheelchair-bound
No family history of brain tumors or seizure disorders

social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, quit 10 years ago, 10 pack-year history
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and two teenage children in a suburban home
social_history.recent_stressors: Dental infection and associated pain over the past month; increased work stress due to end-of-semester grading

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Affect is appropriate but the patient appears anxious. Speech is fluent with normal rate and prosody. No paraphasic errors. Naming intact on confrontation testing. Able to follow three-step commands. Registers 3 of 3 words and recalls 2 of 3 at five minutes. Attention intact on digit span forward (6 digits) and backward (4 digits). Montreal Cognitive Assessment score estimated at 26/30 with minor deficits in delayed recall and abstraction.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light, 3 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam: no papilledema. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch and pinprick bilaterally. Face symmetric at rest and with activation. Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength full bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal throughout. Strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. No pronator drift. No involuntary movements. No asterixis.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception in all four extremities. No extinction to double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady, narrow-based gait. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: During the initial assessment, the patient had one witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately 90 seconds with post-ictal confusion resolving over 15 minutes. Neurological examination documented above was performed 2 hours after seizure resolution and was essentially non-focal. Prior to seizure, the patient's wife reports he has been intermittently 'staring off' for the past week, with episodes lasting 5-10 seconds, which they attributed to fatigue or stress.

patient.chief_complaint: New-onset generalized seizure and progressive headaches for 3 weeks
patient.history_present_illness: This 48-year-old right-handed man was brought to the emergency department by ambulance after a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home. He has no prior history of seizures. According to his wife, the patient has been experiencing progressive headaches over the past three weeks, which he initially attributed to the stress associated with a recent dental abscess and subsequent treatment. The headaches were described as diffuse, worse in the morning upon awakening, and occasionally accompanied by mild nausea. Over-the-counter ibuprofen provided only partial relief. Approximately three weeks prior to this presentation, he was treated for a dental abscess of his right lower second molar with incision and drainage, and was prescribed a 14-day course of amoxicillin-clavulanate, which he completed one week ago. His dentist noted the abscess had been present for at least several days before he sought care, and there was concern about the severity of the infection at the time of drainage. The patient's wife reports that over the past two weeks she has noticed subtle personality changes: he has become more irritable than usual, has been less engaged with the family, and twice forgot to pick up their daughter from soccer practice, which she describes as completely out of character. He also had difficulty preparing a lesson plan for his class last week and needed to ask a colleague for help, which he has never done before. The patient himself downplays these symptoms, attributing them to fatigue and lingering discomfort from the dental procedure. He denies any focal weakness, numbness, visual changes, or difficulty with speech. He denies fever, chills, night sweats, or weight loss. The seizure occurred this morning while he was eating breakfast. His wife witnessed tonic stiffening followed by clonic jerking of all four extremities lasting approximately 90 seconds. He was postictal and confused for approximately 15 minutes afterward. Upon arrival to the emergency department, a non-contrast CT of the head was performed and revealed a ring-enhancing lesion in the right frontotemporal region with surrounding edema and mild mass effect. Given the recent dental infection, infectious etiology was initially raised as a strong diagnostic consideration alongside a neoplastic process.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha rhythm present bilaterally, symmetric and reactive to eye opening
background.asymmetry: Subtle amplitude attenuation over the right hemisphere, most notable in the frontotemporal region

findings.state: Primarily during drowsiness, occasional runs during wakefulness
findings.clinical_correlation: Nonspecific finding consistent with diffuse cerebral dysfunction, may be seen in metabolic encephalopathy, post-ictal state, medication effects, or structural lesions

findings.morphology: Polymorphic, moderate amplitude, intermittent runs lasting 3-8 seconds
findings.clinical_correlation: Bifrontal intermittent slowing may reflect deep midline or bifrontal structural pathology, metabolic encephalopathy, or elevated intracranial pressure. The bifrontal distribution may obscure lateralizing value.

artifacts.description: Intermittent bilateral temporal muscle artifact, partially obscuring temporal activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Mildly increased generalized slowing during hyperventilation, more prominent bifrontally. Patient terminated early due to headache.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Symmetric photic driving at 10-16 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG for a 48-year-old male showing generalized intermittent theta slowing and bifrontal intermittent delta activity without clear lateralizing features. No definite focal epileptiform discharges identified in this recording. The generalized slowing is nonspecific and could reflect post-ictal state, metabolic disturbance, medication effects, or diffuse cerebral dysfunction from any cause. The lack of clearly lateralized findings does not exclude a focal structural lesion, as EEG sensitivity for deep or mesial lesions is limited. Clinical correlation with imaging is essential.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording performed within 4 hours of a generalized tonic-clonic seizure, which may contribute to background slowing. Intermittent muscle artifact over temporal regions limits interpretation of temporal activity. Extended or continuous EEG monitoring is recommended if seizures recur.
recommended_actions: Consider continuous EEG monitoring if further seizures occur
Repeat EEG after post-ictal effects resolve if clinical question remains

findings.location: Right frontotemporal region centered at the junction of the right frontal operculum and superior temporal gyrus, extending into the insula

signal_characteristics.T1: Central hypointensity with peripheral isointense to mildly hyperintense rim
signal_characteristics.T2: Central hyperintensity surrounded by a hypointense rim and extensive surrounding hyperintense edema
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Extensive surrounding hyperintensity consistent with vasogenic edema extending into the right frontal and temporal white matter
signal_characteristics.DWI: Central restricted diffusion within the core of the lesion (ADC map confirms true restriction)
signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Thin, relatively smooth ring enhancement with a small irregular nodular component along the medial wall

findings.mass_effect: Mild rightward-to-leftward subfalcine herniation with 4 mm midline shift. Mild effacement of the right lateral ventricle frontal horn. No uncal herniation.
findings.borders: Ring component is relatively smooth but with focal irregularity medially; surrounding edema is extensive and ill-defined

additional_observations: No additional enhancing lesions identified elsewhere in the brain
Mastoid air cells and paranasal sinuses clear bilaterally
mri.impression: Solitary ring-enhancing lesion in the right frontotemporal region measuring 3.8 cm with central restricted diffusion, surrounding vasogenic edema, and mild midline shift. The imaging characteristics present a diagnostic dilemma: the relatively thin and smooth ring enhancement with central restricted diffusion raises strong concern for a brain abscess, particularly given the clinical history of recent dental infection. However, the focal nodular irregularity along the medial wall and the surrounding edema pattern are also consistent with a high-grade glioma (glioblastoma). A metastatic lesion is less likely given the solitary nature and lack of gray-white junction predilection. Tumefactive demyelination remains a remote possibility given the patient's family history of multiple sclerosis.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Central restricted diffusion (pus), relatively smooth thin ring enhancement, and clinical history of recent dental abscess are classic features of pyogenic brain abscess
Focal nodular irregularity of the enhancing rim, extensive surrounding edema, and location are compatible. However, the relatively thin ring and central DWI restriction are atypical for GBM.
Solitary lesion without gray-white junction predilection; no known primary malignancy. Cannot be fully excluded.
Family history of MS raises this consideration. Would expect open-ring enhancement and less mass effect relative to size. Age and sex are atypical.

recommended_actions: MR spectroscopy to differentiate abscess from neoplasm
MR perfusion to evaluate cerebral blood volume in the enhancing rim
Consider stereotactic aspiration/biopsy for definitive diagnosis
Infectious disease consultation given clinical history
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to evaluate for primary malignancy if metastasis is considered

CBC.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated; may reflect recent dental infection, stress response from seizure, or be a nonspecific finding. Does not clearly support or refute abscess vs neoplasm.
Mildly elevated neutrophils consistent with recent infection or stress response

BMP.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated; consistent with known diet-controlled type 2 diabetes and physiologic stress response from seizure

Inflammatory markers.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated; could reflect residual inflammation from dental abscess, an active intracranial abscess, or a nonspecific response to seizure or malignancy. Does not clearly differentiate abscess from tumor.
Mildly elevated; nonspecific marker, may be seen in both infection and malignancy
Normal procalcitonin argues against active systemic bacterial infection but does not exclude a walled-off abscess

Other.clinical_significance: Consistent with known diet-controlled diabetes; currently in prediabetic/early diabetic range
Negative blood cultures make active bacteremia unlikely but do not exclude a walled-off intracranial abscess

labs.interpretation: Mildly elevated WBC (11.2) with mild neutrophilia, mildly elevated CRP (1.8) and ESR (28), but normal procalcitonin and negative blood cultures. The inflammatory markers are equivocal and could reflect residual inflammation from the recently treated dental abscess, a walled-off intracranial abscess, or a nonspecific tumor-associated inflammatory response. The mildly elevated glucose and HbA1c are consistent with known diabetes. Coagulation panel is normal, suitable for pre-surgical baseline. Hepatic and renal function are normal, suitable as baseline for potential chemotherapy. Overall, the lab findings do not clearly differentiate between an infectious and neoplastic intracranial process.

signal_characteristics.rCBV_enhancing_rim: Elevated (rCBV ratio 2.8 relative to contralateral normal-appearing white matter)
signal_characteristics.rCBV_perilesional_edema: Mildly elevated in the immediate perilesional zone (rCBV ratio 1.4), suggesting infiltrative tumor beyond the enhancing margin
signal_characteristics.permeability: Elevated Ktrans in the enhancing rim, consistent with blood-brain barrier disruption

findings.borders: Enhancing rim with elevated rCBV; perilesional edema shows mildly elevated perfusion suggesting tumor infiltration

additional_observations: The elevated rCBV in the enhancing rim (ratio 2.8) is above the threshold typically used to distinguish high-grade neoplasm (>1.75) from abscess or low-grade glioma
Mildly elevated perfusion in the perilesional edema zone is more consistent with infiltrative tumor (glioma) than with pure vasogenic edema surrounding an abscess
An abscess would typically show low rCBV throughout, including the enhancing wall
output.impression: MR perfusion demonstrates elevated relative cerebral blood volume (rCBV ratio 2.8) in the enhancing rim of the right frontotemporal lesion, strongly favoring a high-grade neoplastic process over a pyogenic brain abscess. The mildly elevated perfusion in the perilesional zone further supports tumor infiltration beyond the enhancing margins, a hallmark of high-grade glioma. These perfusion characteristics significantly shift the differential toward glioblastoma.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Elevated rCBV in the enhancing rim (>1.75) and elevated perilesional perfusion are characteristic of high-grade glioma with neoangiogenesis
Brain abscesses typically show low rCBV in both the enhancing wall and center due to lack of neoangiogenesis. The elevated rCBV makes abscess unlikely.

recommended_actions: MR spectroscopy for additional metabolic characterization
Stereotactic biopsy for definitive tissue diagnosis

findings.location: Right frontotemporal lesion, voxels placed in the enhancing rim, central core, and perilesional tissue

signal_characteristics.enhancing_rim_lipid_lactate: Prominent lipid and lactate peaks present
signal_characteristics.central_core: Dominant lipid and lactate peaks with negligible choline or NAA, consistent with necrosis
signal_characteristics.perilesional_tissue: Mildly elevated choline with reduced NAA (Cho/NAA ratio 1.8), suggesting tumor infiltration into perilesional tissue
signal_characteristics.amino_acids: No convincing amino acid peaks (no acetate or succinate peaks)

additional_observations: The spectroscopic pattern in the enhancing rim (markedly elevated choline, reduced NAA, lipid/lactate) is characteristic of a high-grade glioma with high cellularity and membrane turnover
Absence of amino acid peaks (acetate, succinate) argues against a pyogenic abscess, where these peaks from bacterial metabolism are typically present
Elevated Cho/NAA ratio in the perilesional tissue supports infiltrative tumor extending beyond the enhancing margin, consistent with glioma rather than abscess or metastasis
Central necrosis with dominant lipid/lactate peaks is typical of glioblastoma
output.impression: MR spectroscopy demonstrates a metabolic profile highly consistent with high-grade glioma (glioblastoma): markedly elevated choline with reduced NAA in the enhancing rim, lipid and lactate peaks indicating necrosis centrally, and critically, the absence of amino acid peaks (acetate, succinate) that would be expected in a pyogenic brain abscess. Additionally, the elevated Cho/NAA ratio in perilesional tissue suggests tumor infiltration beyond the enhancing boundary. These spectroscopic findings, combined with the perfusion data, strongly support a diagnosis of glioblastoma rather than brain abscess.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Classic spectroscopic pattern: elevated choline, reduced NAA, lipid/lactate peaks, no amino acid peaks, and perilesional infiltrative pattern
Absence of amino acid peaks (acetate, succinate, valine, leucine, isoleucine) that are pathognomonic for pyogenic abscess

recommended_actions: Proceed with stereotactic biopsy for definitive histological and molecular diagnosis
Neurosurgical planning for maximal safe resection

CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.value: Clear, no pulmonary nodules or masses. No pleural effusion.
CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.clinical_significance: No evidence of primary lung malignancy

CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.value: No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Heart size normal.
Homogeneous enhancement, no focal hepatic lesions. Mild hepatic steatosis.
CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.clinical_significance: Mild fatty liver consistent with metabolic syndrome

CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.value: Normal size bilaterally, no masses or hydronephrosis. Simple 1.2 cm right renal cyst.
CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.clinical_significance: Simple renal cyst, incidental finding of no clinical significance

CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.value: No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis
No suspicious lytic or blastic osseous lesions

output.interpretation: No evidence of primary extracranial malignancy or metastatic disease on CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis. This effectively rules out the brain lesion being a solitary brain metastasis from a common primary site (lung, kidney, colon, melanoma). The findings support a primary brain tumor as the diagnosis. Incidental findings include mild hepatic steatosis and a simple right renal cyst, both clinically insignificant.

Neuropathology.value: Densely cellular glial neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity (>10 mitoses per 10 HPF), microvascular proliferation, and areas of palisading necrosis. Consistent with glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4.
Neuropathology.clinical_significance: Definitive diagnosis of glioblastoma, the most aggressive primary brain tumor
Absence of IDH1 R132H mutation; IDH-wildtype glioblastoma carries a worse prognosis than IDH-mutant tumors
Unmethylated MGMT promoter predicts reduced response to temozolomide chemotherapy; however, standard Stupp protocol is still recommended
Absence of 1p/19q codeletion excludes oligodendroglioma; consistent with astrocytic lineage
TERT promoter mutation is characteristic of glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype
EGFR amplification is common in IDH-wildtype glioblastoma and is one of the molecular criteria for WHO grade 4 classification

Neuropathology.value: Combined whole chromosome 7 gain and chromosome 10 loss
Neuropathology.clinical_significance: Another molecular hallmark of IDH-wildtype glioblastoma
High proliferation index consistent with high-grade malignancy
No evidence of bacterial infection; rules out abscess

output.interpretation: Stereotactic biopsy definitively confirms glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4. Key molecular features include unmethylated MGMT promoter, TERT promoter mutation, EGFR amplification, and combined +7/-10 chromosomal changes. IDH1/IDH2 are wildtype and 1p/19q codeletion is absent, consistent with a de novo glioblastoma of astrocytic lineage. The unmethylated MGMT status predicts reduced benefit from temozolomide, though the standard Stupp protocol (maximal safe resection followed by concurrent radiation and temozolomide) remains the recommended treatment. Importantly, Gram stain and cultures of the aspirate are negative, definitively excluding a brain abscess.

findings.type: Functional MRI for pre-surgical motor and language mapping
findings.location: Right frontotemporal lesion in relation to eloquent cortex

signal_characteristics.motor_cortex: Right primary motor cortex (hand area) activation identified along the precentral gyrus, displaced posteriorly and medially by the tumor. Distance from tumor margin to motor activation: approximately 8 mm.
signal_characteristics.broca_area: Left Broca area activation robust and normal in location. No right hemispheric language lateralization detected.
signal_characteristics.supplementary_motor_area: Right SMA activation partially involved by tumor-associated edema

findings.mass_effect: Tumor displaces the right motor cortex posteriorly
findings.borders: Tumor margin approaches but does not clearly infiltrate the primary motor strip based on fMRI activation

additional_observations: Language function is strongly left-lateralized, reducing the risk of language deficits from surgery on this right-sided lesion
The right motor cortex is displaced but appears functionally intact based on activation patterns
The proximity of the tumor to the motor strip (8 mm) warrants intraoperative cortical stimulation mapping
DTI tractography shows the right corticospinal tract is displaced medially but intact, with no frank invasion
output.impression: Functional MRI demonstrates strongly left-lateralized language function, meaning the right frontotemporal tumor resection carries low risk for language deficits. The right primary motor cortex is displaced approximately 8 mm from the tumor margin but remains functionally active. The right corticospinal tract is displaced but intact on DTI. Maximal safe resection appears feasible with intraoperative monitoring, though the proximity to motor cortex necessitates awake craniotomy or cortical stimulation mapping to minimize the risk of contralateral motor deficits.
recommended_actions: Intraoperative cortical stimulation mapping recommended given proximity to motor cortex
Consider awake craniotomy for motor mapping
Neuronavigation using fMRI and DTI overlay during surgical planning
Pre-operative discussion of risk of contralateral (left-sided) motor deficits

Infectious Disease Consultation.value: Patient was evaluated for possible intracranial abscess in the setting of recent dental abscess. The temporal relationship between the dental infection and discovery of the intracranial lesion is concerning for hematogenous or contiguous spread. However, several features argue against abscess: (1) the patient completed a full course of appropriate antibiotics with clinical resolution of the dental infection, (2) blood cultures are negative, (3) procalcitonin is normal, (4) the patient is afebrile, and (5) MRI perfusion and spectroscopy findings favor neoplasm over abscess. While empiric antibiotics covering CNS penetration could be considered (e.g., ceftriaxone + metronidazole), the advanced imaging characteristics strongly favor a neoplastic process. Stereotactic biopsy with cultures is the most reliable means to definitively differentiate.
Infectious Disease Consultation.clinical_significance: ID consultation supports pursuing tissue diagnosis via biopsy rather than empiric antibiotic therapy alone

Infectious Disease Consultation.value: Recommend stereotactic biopsy with specimen sent for both pathology and microbiology (Gram stain, aerobic and anaerobic cultures, fungal cultures). If biopsy is delayed, consider empiric ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h plus metronidazole 500mg IV q8h pending tissue diagnosis. Dexamethasone may be used for edema but note it can reduce enhancement of both abscess and tumor.

output.interpretation: Infectious disease consultation acknowledges the clinical concern for brain abscess given the recent dental infection but notes that the overall clinical picture and advanced imaging features are more suggestive of a neoplastic process. Tissue diagnosis is recommended as the definitive next step, with cultures sent alongside pathology specimens.

Dental Consultation.value: Right lower second molar (#31) extraction site well-healed with no residual swelling, erythema, or purulent drainage. Surrounding gingiva healthy. No sinus tracts. No other dental caries or periapical pathology identified on panoramic radiograph.
Dental Consultation.clinical_significance: Dental infection has fully resolved. No current source of ongoing odontogenic infection that could seed intracranial abscess.

Dental Consultation.value: Post-extraction site #31 shows normal healing. No periapical radiolucencies. No residual root fragments.

output.interpretation: Dental consultation confirms complete resolution of the prior dental abscess with no evidence of ongoing odontogenic infection. The extraction site is well-healed. This finding, while not definitive, reduces the likelihood that the intracranial lesion represents a brain abscess secondary to hematogenous spread from a dental source, as there is no active source of infection.

differential.likelihood: Moderate (initial leading differential due to clinical history)
differential.key_distinguishing: Central restricted diffusion and smooth ring enhancement initially mimic abscess. However, MR perfusion shows elevated rCBV in the enhancing rim (abscess would show low rCBV), MR spectroscopy shows no amino acid peaks (abscess would show acetate, succinate), dental infection has fully resolved, blood cultures are negative, patient is afebrile, and procalcitonin is normal.

Solitary lesion without gray-white junction predilection, no known primary malignancy, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis negative for extracranial primary. Metastases tend to be more spherical with sharper margins.

CNS lymphoma typically shows homogeneous enhancement, periventricular location, and restricted diffusion throughout (not just centrally). Patient is immunocompetent. Lymphoma often dramatically regresses with corticosteroids.

Family history of MS raises consideration but patient is a 48-year-old male with no personal MS history. Tumefactive MS typically shows open-ring enhancement, less mass effect relative to lesion size, and would be expected in a younger patient.

Low-grade gliomas are typically non-enhancing, FLAIR-bright without necrosis, and present in younger patients. The ring enhancement, necrosis, and high rCBV are all characteristic of high-grade disease.

optimal_actions.action: Stabilize the patient post-seizure with IV lorazepam if actively seizing, followed by loading dose of levetiracetam 1000-1500 mg IV for seizure prophylaxis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Seizure cessation and prevention of recurrent seizures

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive labs: CBC, BMP, hepatic panel, coagulation studies, CRP, ESR, procalcitonin, blood cultures, HbA1c, LDH
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mildly elevated WBC and inflammatory markers; equivocal findings that do not clearly distinguish infection from neoplasm

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Ring-enhancing lesion with central restricted diffusion and surrounding edema creating diagnostic dilemma between abscess and high-grade glioma

optimal_actions.action: Obtain routine EEG to characterize seizure focus and assess for subclinical seizure activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Generalized slowing without clear focal features, complicating localization

optimal_actions.action: Initiate dexamethasone 10 mg IV bolus followed by 4 mg IV q6h for vasogenic edema and mass effect
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduction in perilesional edema and improvement in headache and mass effect symptoms

optimal_actions.action: Order MR perfusion to evaluate cerebral blood volume in the enhancing rim to differentiate neoplasm from abscess
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated rCBV in enhancing rim (ratio 2.8), strongly favoring neoplasm over abscess

optimal_actions.action: Order MR spectroscopy to evaluate metabolic profile of the lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated choline, reduced NAA, lipid/lactate peaks without amino acid peaks, confirming neoplastic over infectious etiology

optimal_actions.action: Request infectious disease consultation given the confounding dental infection history
optimal_actions.expected_finding: ID acknowledges concern but notes imaging features favor neoplasm; recommends tissue diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis to rule out primary extracranial malignancy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No evidence of extracranial primary malignancy, supporting a primary brain tumor diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Proceed with stereotactic biopsy with specimens sent for both neuropathology and microbiology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4 on histology; negative cultures definitively excluding abscess

optimal_actions.action: Obtain functional MRI for pre-surgical language and motor mapping given proximity to eloquent cortex
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left-lateralized language, motor cortex displaced but functionally intact, maximal safe resection is feasible

optimal_actions.action: Plan maximal safe surgical resection with intraoperative cortical stimulation mapping, followed by Stupp protocol (concurrent temozolomide and radiation therapy)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Surgical and adjuvant treatment plan established via multidisciplinary tumor board

critical_actions: Do not assume the ring-enhancing lesion is a brain abscess based solely on the dental infection history -- advanced imaging (perfusion, spectroscopy) is essential to differentiate abscess from glioblastoma
Order MR perfusion and MR spectroscopy to resolve the abscess-vs-tumor diagnostic dilemma before committing to a treatment plan
Obtain tissue diagnosis via stereotactic biopsy with specimens sent for BOTH pathology AND microbiology to definitively differentiate the two main diagnostic considerations
Initiate anti-seizure medication after the first unprovoked seizure in the setting of a structural brain lesion
Administer dexamethasone for symptomatic vasogenic edema and mass effect
Request molecular profiling (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q, TERT, EGFR) on the biopsy specimen to guide treatment planning
Perform metastatic workup (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) to rule out a primary extracranial malignancy
contraindicated_actions: Treating empirically for brain abscess with prolonged antibiotics without obtaining tissue diagnosis, which would delay appropriate oncological treatment
Performing burr hole aspiration/drainage as if this were a confirmed abscess without first obtaining advanced imaging to characterize the lesion
Discharging the patient with outpatient follow-up given the presence of a ring-enhancing mass lesion with midline shift
Withholding corticosteroids in the setting of significant vasogenic edema and mass effect
Administering carbamazepine or phenytoin as initial anti-seizure medication in a patient who may require temozolomide, as these are strong CYP450 inducers that reduce temozolomide efficacy
Performing lumbar puncture in the setting of significant mass effect and midline shift due to risk of uncal herniation
key_reasoning_points: The recent dental abscess is the central confounding factor in this diagnostic puzzle -- it creates a plausible mechanism for hematogenous spread of bacteria to the brain, making brain abscess the initial leading differential

Central restricted diffusion on DWI is present in both brain abscess (pus) and the necrotic core of glioblastoma (tumor necrosis), making this a misleading finding that does not differentiate the two diagnoses on standard MRI alone
MR perfusion is the key discriminating study: elevated rCBV in the enhancing rim strongly favors neoplastic neoangiogenesis (glioblastoma) over the avascular capsule of a brain abscess

MR spectroscopy provides additional discriminatory value: the presence of amino acid peaks (acetate, succinate) is nearly pathognomonic for pyogenic abscess, while elevated choline with reduced NAA favors tumor. Their absence in this case further supports glioblastoma
The relatively thin and smooth ring enhancement initially favors abscess over glioblastoma, but the focal nodular irregularity on the medial wall is a subtle clue pointing toward neoplasm

The mildly elevated inflammatory markers (WBC, CRP, ESR) are deliberately ambiguous -- they could reflect either the recent dental infection or a nonspecific tumor-associated inflammatory response, preventing definitive differentiation by labs alone
The normal procalcitonin and negative blood cultures are subtle clues against an active systemic bacterial infection, but they do not exclude a walled-off intracranial abscess
Family history of multiple sclerosis introduces tumefactive demyelination as a potential red herring, further complicating the differential

metadata.author: NeuroBench Generator
metadata.diagnostic_challenge_notes: This case is designed as a diagnostic puzzle where a recent dental abscess creates a compelling but misleading narrative for brain abscess when a ring-enhancing lesion is discovered. The central restricted diffusion on standard MRI, mildly elevated inflammatory markers, and clinical history of odontogenic infection all initially point toward abscess. Only advanced imaging modalities (MR perfusion and spectroscopy) and ultimately tissue diagnosis can definitively resolve the diagnostic dilemma. The EEG is deliberately non-lateralizing, adding to the diagnostic uncertainty. An experienced clinician must resist premature closure on the abscess diagnosis and pursue the full imaging and tissue workup.
